PE.definition: Pulmonary Embolism: a blood clot lodged in the pulmonary arteries, often from a DVT. A differential diagnosis for acute dyspnea and chest pain in ME/CFS patients.

TBI.label: TBI
TBI.definition: Traumatic Brain Injury: brain damage from external force. TBI can trigger ME/CFS-like symptoms; post-concussion syndrome shares features with ME/CFS.

SCI.label: SCI
SCI.definition: Spinal Cord Injury: damage to the spinal cord from trauma or disease. SCI-associated fatigue and autonomic dysfunction overlap with ME/CFS symptoms.

TMJ.label: TMJ
TMJ.definition: Temporomandibular Joint dysfunction: pain and dysfunction of the jaw joint and muscles. Reported comorbidity in ME/CFS/fibromyalgia, possibly linked to hypermobility and central sensitization.

NETosis.label: NETosis
NETosis.definition: NETosis: a form of programmed neutrophil death releasing neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) — webs of DNA, histones, and antimicrobial proteins. NETosis is a key innate immune defense; excessive NETosis has been implicated in autoimmune and inflammatory conditions that overlap with ME/CFS.

DAMP.label: DAMP
DAMP.definition: Damage-Associated Molecular Pattern: endogenous molecules (HMGB1, ATP, HSPs, mtDNA, S100 proteins) released from stressed or dying cells that activate innate immune receptors. Chronic DAMP release from cellular stress may sustain sterile inflammation in ME/CFS.

PAMP.label: PAMP
PAMP.definition: Pathogen-Associated Molecular Pattern: conserved microbial molecules (LPS, flagellin, viral dsRNA) detected by pattern recognition receptors (TLRs, RIG-I, NLRs). Persistent PAMP exposure from chronic infections or leaky gut may drive immune activation in ME/CFS.

TIBC.label: TIBC
TIBC.definition: Total Iron-Binding Capacity: a measure of the blood's capacity to bind iron with transferrin. Elevated TIBC indicates iron deficiency; relevant to ME/CFS fatigue differential diagnosis.

TSAT.label: TSAT
TSAT.definition: Transferrin Saturation: the ratio of serum iron to TIBC, expressed as a percentage. Low TSAT (<20%) indicates iron deficiency, a common and treatable cause of fatigue that must be excluded in ME/CFS.

Ferritin.label: Ferritin
Ferritin.definition: Ferritin: the primary intracellular iron storage protein; serum ferritin reflects body iron stores. Low ferritin indicates iron deficiency anemia. Ferritin can be falsely elevated in inflammation (acute phase reactant).

HDL.label: HDL
HDL.definition: High-Density Lipoprotein: the 'good' cholesterol particle mediating reverse cholesterol transport. HDL particle function (not just concentration) may be impaired in ME/CFS due to oxidative modification.

LDL.label: LDL
LDL.definition: Low-Density Lipoprotein: the 'bad' cholesterol particle carrying cholesterol to tissues. Oxidized LDL (oxLDL) is a marker of oxidative stress that may be elevated in ME/CFS.

Lp(a).label: Lp(a)
Lp(a).definition: Lipoprotein(a): an LDL-like particle with apolipoprotein(a) covalently bound to apoB. Genetically determined; elevated Lp(a) is an independent cardiovascular risk factor.

Homocysteine.label: Homocysteine
Homocysteine.definition: Homocysteine: a sulfur-containing amino acid intermediate in methionine metabolism. Elevated homocysteine indicates impaired methylation (B12/folate deficiency, MTHFR variants) and is an independent cardiovascular risk factor; reported elevated in some ME/CFS cohorts.

HbA1c.label: HbA1c
HbA1c.definition: Glycated Hemoglobin A1c: reflects average blood glucose over the preceding 2–3 months. Used to screen for diabetes, which can cause fatigue resembling ME/CFS and must be excluded.

HOMA-IR.label: HOMA-IR
HOMA-IR.definition: Homeostatic Model Assessment of Insulin Resistance: calculated from fasting glucose and insulin. Assesses insulin resistance; metabolic studies suggest insulin resistance may be present in a subset of ME/CFS patients.

OGTT.label: OGTT
OGTT.definition: Oral Glucose Tolerance Test: measures the body's ability to handle a glucose load over 2 hours. Used to detect impaired glucose tolerance and diabetes; both can cause fatigue and must be excluded in ME/CFS assessment.

PT.label: PT
PT.definition: Prothrombin Time: a coagulation test measuring the extrinsic and common pathways. INR is derived from PT. Generally normal in ME/CFS unless anticoagulated or liver disease present.

INR.label: INR
INR.definition: International Normalized Ratio: a standardized PT ratio used to monitor warfarin therapy and assess liver synthetic function. Normal in ME/CFS.

aPTT.label: aPTT
aPTT.definition: Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time: a coagulation test measuring the intrinsic and common pathways. Used to screen for bleeding disorders and monitor heparin therapy.

D-dimer.label: D-dimer
D-dimer.definition: D-dimer: a fibrin degradation product indicating coagulation and fibrinolysis. Elevated in thrombosis, inflammation, and infection. Elevated D-dimer in Long COVID/ME/CFS may indicate persistent microvascular coagulation.

GGT.label: GGT
GGT.definition: Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase: a liver enzyme elevated in biliary disease, alcohol use, and oxidative stress. GGT may also serve as a marker of glutathione depletion; elevated GGT in ME/CFS could reflect oxidative stress.

ALP.label: ALP
ALP.definition: Alkaline Phosphatase: an enzyme from liver, bone, and placenta. Elevated ALP requires differential diagnosis (liver disease, bone disease). Generally normal in ME/CFS.

BUN.label: BUN
BUN.definition: Blood Urea Nitrogen: a marker of kidney function and protein metabolism. BUN may be elevated in dehydration (common in ME/CFS with POTS due to low fluid intake) or kidney disease.

Cr.label: Cr
Cr.definition: Creatinine: a waste product of muscle metabolism, excreted by the kidneys. Serum creatinine is the most common kidney function marker; used with cystatin C for eGFR calculation. Low creatinine may indicate low muscle mass in severe ME/CFS.

PTH.label: PTH
PTH.definition: Parathyroid Hormone: regulates calcium and phosphate homeostasis. Elevated PTH may reflect vitamin D deficiency, common in housebound ME/CFS patients with limited sun exposure.

B12.label: B12
B12.definition: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin): essential for methylation, DNA synthesis, and neurological function. B12 deficiency causes fatigue, neuropathy, and cognitive dysfunction indistinguishable from ME/CFS and must be excluded.

Folate.label: Folate
Folate.definition: Vitamin B9: essential for DNA synthesis, methylation, and red blood cell production. Folate deficiency causes fatigue and macrocytic anemia; must be excluded in ME/CFS workup. MTHFR polymorphisms may impair folate utilization.

FODMAP.label: FODMAP
FODMAP.definition: Fermentable Oligosaccharides, Disaccharides, Monosaccharides, and Polyols: short-chain carbohydrates poorly absorbed in the small intestine, fermented by gut bacteria causing bloating and gas. Low-FODMAP diet is used in ME/CFS with IBS/SIBO comorbidity to reduce GI symptoms.

AIP.label: AIP
AIP.definition: Autoimmune Protocol diet: an elimination diet removing potentially immunogenic foods (grains, legumes, nightshades, dairy, eggs, nuts, seeds) followed by structured reintroduction. Used experimentally in ME/CFS patients with suspected autoimmune or food-sensitivity components.

AHR.label: AHR
AHR.definition: Aryl Hydrocarbon Receptor: a ligand-activated transcription factor sensing environmental toxins and tryptophan metabolites (kynurenine). AHR activation by kynurenine shifts T-cell differentiation toward Treg vs Th17; the IDO-KYN-AHR axis links tryptophan metabolism to immune regulation in ME/CFS.

FXR.label: FXR
FXR.definition: Farnesoid X Receptor: a nuclear bile acid receptor regulating bile acid synthesis, lipid metabolism, and gut-liver signaling. FXR dysfunction may link gut microbiome alterations to metabolic abnormalities in ME/CFS.

VDR.label: VDR
VDR.definition: Vitamin D Receptor: a nuclear receptor activated by 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3. VDR regulates calcium homeostasis, immune function, and antimicrobial peptide production; vitamin D deficiency and VDR polymorphisms have been studied in ME/CFS.

COVID.label: COVID
COVID.definition: Coronavirus Disease 2019: the illness caused by SARS-CoV-2. COVID-19 is a major infectious trigger for post-infectious ME/CFS (Long COVID/PASC), creating the largest-ever cohort of post-viral ME/CFS patients.

RECOVER.label: RECOVER
RECOVER.definition: Researching COVID to Enhance Recovery: the NIH's $1.15 billion Long COVID research initiative. RECOVER findings have strong implications for ME/CFS given the substantial overlap between Long COVID and ME/CFS.

oxidative stress.label: oxidative stress
oxidative stress.definition: An imbalance between reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and antioxidant defenses, leading to oxidative damage of lipids, proteins, and DNA. One of the most consistently replicated biological abnormalities in ME/CFS, driving the NO/ONOO cycle hypothesis.

neuroinflammation.label: neuroinflammation
neuroinflammation.definition: Inflammatory activation of microglia and astrocytes within the CNS, producing cytokines, chemokines, and reactive species. PET imaging and CSF studies confirm neuroinflammation in ME/CFS, particularly in the brainstem, thalamus, and cortex.

central sensitization.label: central sensitization
central sensitization.definition: Increased responsiveness of nociceptive neurons in the CNS to normal or subthreshold afferent input. Explains widespread pain, allodynia, and hyperalgesia in ME/CFS and fibromyalgia beyond what peripheral pathology would predict.

excitotoxicity.label: excitotoxicity
excitotoxicity.definition: Neuronal damage caused by excessive activation of glutamate receptors (NMDA, AMPA), leading to calcium overload, mitochondrial failure, and cell death. Driven by elevated quinolinic acid (QUIN) in the kynurenine pathway; implicated in ME/CFS cognitive dysfunction.

metabolic inflexibility.label: metabolic inflexibility
metabolic inflexibility.definition: Impaired ability to switch between glucose and fatty acid oxidation in response to demand. A core metabolic phenotype in ME/CFS: cells remain locked in glycolysis even when fatty acid oxidation would be more efficient, contributing to early fatigue and lactate accumulation.

mitochondrial dysfunction.label: mitochondrial dysfunction
mitochondrial dysfunction.definition: Impaired mitochondrial ATP production due to electron transport chain defects, reduced mitochondrial biogenesis, oxidative damage, or substrate limitation. A leading hypothesis for the profound fatigability and PEM in ME/CFS.

endothelial dysfunction.label: endothelial dysfunction
endothelial dysfunction.definition: Impaired endothelium-dependent vasodilation due to reduced nitric oxide bioavailability, increased oxidative stress, and inflammation. Contributes to microvascular hypoperfusion and orthostatic intolerance in ME/CFS.

dysbiosis.label: dysbiosis
dysbiosis.definition: An imbalance in the composition, diversity, or function of the gut microbiome. Gut dysbiosis is consistently reported in ME/CFS, with reduced microbial diversity and shifts in short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) producers, linking to immune and metabolic dysfunction.

leaky gut.label: leaky gut
leaky gut.definition: Increased intestinal permeability allowing bacterial products (LPS, flagellin) to translocate from the gut lumen into systemic circulation. Intestinal permeability may drive chronic low-grade immune activation ('metabolic endotoxemia') in ME/CFS.

allostatic load.label: allostatic load
allostatic load.definition: The cumulative physiological wear and tear from chronic stress or repeated adaptive responses. ME/CFS can be framed as a state of decompensated allostatic overload where the body's regulatory systems (HPA axis, ANS, immune) lose their adaptive capacity.

sickness behavior.label: sickness behavior
sickness behavior.definition: A coordinated behavioral and physiological response to infection/inflammation: fatigue, anhedonia, social withdrawal, hyperalgesia, and cognitive slowing. Mediated by pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) acting on the brain; ME/CFS resembles a chronic sickness behavior state.

microglial priming.label: microglial priming
microglial priming.definition: A state where microglia become hypersensitive to subsequent stimuli after an initial insult, producing exaggerated inflammatory responses. May explain why ME/CFS patients experience symptom flares from minor triggers that would not affect healthy individuals.

glycolysis.label: glycolysis
glycolysis.definition: The cytoplasmic breakdown of glucose to pyruvate, producing 2 ATP and NADH. In ME/CFS, impaired pyruvate dehydrogenase activity may shunt metabolism toward anaerobic glycolysis, producing lactate even at rest.

gluconeogenesis.label: gluconeogenesis
gluconeogenesis.definition: The synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors (lactate, amino acids, glycerol) in the liver and kidneys. Critical for maintaining blood glucose during fasting; may be dysregulated in ME/CFS contributing to hypoglycemia-like symptoms.

β-oxidation.label: β-oxidation
β-oxidation.definition: The mitochondrial breakdown of fatty acids to acetyl-CoA, producing NADH and FADH₂ for the electron transport chain. Impaired β-oxidation in ME/CFS forces reliance on glycolysis, reducing total ATP yield and accelerating fatigue.

autophagy.label: autophagy
autophagy.definition: The cellular self-digestion process that degrades damaged organelles, protein aggregates, and pathogens, recycling components for reuse. Impaired autophagy in ME/CFS may accumulate mitochondrial damage and protein aggregates, perpetuating cellular dysfunction.

mitophagy.label: mitophagy
mitophagy.definition: The selective autophagic degradation of damaged or dysfunctional mitochondria. Mitophagy failure in ME/CFS leads to accumulation of defective mitochondria, impaired energy production, and increased ROS generation.

apoptosis.label: apoptosis
apoptosis.definition: Programmed cell death — a controlled, non-inflammatory form of cellular suicide. Increased lymphocyte apoptosis has been reported in ME/CFS; failure to clear apoptotic debris may contribute to autoimmunity.

ferroptosis.label: ferroptosis
ferroptosis.definition: An iron-dependent form of regulated cell death driven by lipid peroxidation. Ferroptosis is linked to neurodegenerative diseases and may contribute to neuronal loss in ME/CFS given the oxidative stress and iron dysregulation observed.

pyroptosis.label: pyroptosis
pyroptosis.definition: A highly inflammatory programmed cell death mediated by gasdermin D pores, releasing IL-1β, IL-18, and DAMPs. Driven by inflammasome activation (NLRP3, AIM2); pyroptosis may contribute to sustained inflammation in ME/CFS.

necroptosis.label: necroptosis
necroptosis.definition: A regulated form of necrosis mediated by RIPK1/RIPK3/MLKL, releasing cellular contents that trigger inflammation. Necroptosis is distinct from apoptosis and pyroptosis; may be activated in ME/CFS under conditions where apoptosis is inhibited.

senescence.label: senescence
senescence.definition: A state of irreversible cell cycle arrest with a pro-inflammatory secretory phenotype (SASP). Accelerated cellular senescence has been proposed in ME/CFS based on epigenetic clock studies and may explain premature biological aging.

SASP.label: SASP
SASP.definition: Senescence-Associated Secretory Phenotype: the cocktail of pro-inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, growth factors, and proteases secreted by senescent cells. SASP may contribute to systemic inflammation and tissue dysfunction in ME/CFS.

angiogenesis.label: angiogenesis
angiogenesis.definition: The formation of new blood vessels from existing vasculature. Dysregulated angiogenesis via VEGF may contribute to microvascular abnormalities observed in ME/CFS.

vasodilation.label: vasodilation
vasodilation.definition: The widening of blood vessels via relaxation of smooth muscle. Impaired NO-mediated vasodilation in ME/CFS contributes to microvascular dysfunction and orthostatic intolerance.

PPV.label: PPV
PPV.definition: Positive Predictive Value: the probability that a person with a positive test result actually has the disease. PPV depends on disease prevalence; low PPV in rare diseases means many positive results are false positives, a key limitation in ME/CFS biomarker studies.

NPV.label: NPV
NPV.definition: Negative Predictive Value: the probability that a person with a negative test result truly does not have the disease. Important for ruling out ME/CFS mimics via diagnostic testing.

NNT.label: NNT
NNT.definition: Number Needed to Treat: the number of patients who must be treated for one to benefit. High NNTs in ME/CFS treatment trials reflect the heterogeneous response to any single intervention.

off-label.label: off-label
off-label.definition: Use of a medication for an indication, dose, route, or population not approved by regulatory agencies. Essentially all pharmacological treatments for ME/CFS are off-label, as no drug has FDA/EMA approval specifically for ME/CFS.

contraindication.label: contraindication
contraindication.definition: A condition or factor that makes a particular treatment inadvisable. Graded exercise therapy (GET) is contraindicated in ME/CFS per NICE 2021 guidelines due to risk of harm via PEM exacerbation.

half-life.label: half-life
half-life.definition: The time required for the plasma concentration of a drug to decrease by 50%. Longer half-life drugs (e.g., fluoxetine, ~4–16 days for active metabolite) require cautious dosing in ME/CFS patients who may have altered drug metabolism.

bioavailability.label: bioavailability
bioavailability.definition: The fraction of an administered drug dose that reaches systemic circulation unchanged. Oral bioavailability may be altered in ME/CFS due to GI dysmotility, SIBO, or altered first-pass metabolism.

prodrug.label: prodrug
prodrug.definition: An inactive compound that requires metabolic conversion in the body to become pharmacologically active. Valacyclovir (prodrug of acyclovir), codeine (prodrug of morphine), and lisdexamfetamine (prodrug of dextroamphetamine) require functional hepatic metabolism, which may be altered in ME/CFS.

CYP450.label: CYP450
CYP450.definition: Cytochrome P450: a superfamily of liver enzymes (CYP3A4, CYP2D6, CYP2C19, etc.) metabolizing most drugs. CYP polymorphisms and altered CYP activity in ME/CFS may explain variable drug responses and adverse reactions.

therapeutic index.label: therapeutic index
therapeutic index.definition: The ratio between toxic and therapeutic doses of a drug. Drugs with a narrow therapeutic index (lithium, digoxin, warfarin) require careful monitoring in ME/CFS patients who may have altered drug sensitivity.

locus coeruleus.label: locus coeruleus
locus coeruleus.definition: A small nucleus in the brainstem that is the primary source of norepinephrine in the brain. The LC regulates arousal, attention, stress responses, and autonomic tone; LC dysfunction is central to the 'tired but wired' state and hyperadrenergic symptoms in ME/CFS.

ventral tegmental area.label: Ventral Tegmental Area
ventral tegmental area.definition: The VTA is a midbrain nucleus containing dopamine neurons projecting to the nucleus accumbens, prefrontal cortex, and amygdala via the mesolimbic and mesocortical pathways. VTA dopamine dysfunction links to fatigue, anhedonia, and motivational deficits in ME/CFS.

nucleus accumbens.label: nucleus accumbens
nucleus accumbens.definition: A key structure in the ventral striatum mediating reward, motivation, and effort-based decision making. Reduced nucleus accumbens activation in ME/CFS may explain the perceived effort cost of cognitive and physical tasks.

prefrontal cortex.label: prefrontal cortex
prefrontal cortex.definition: The anterior part of the frontal lobes mediating executive function, working memory, decision making, and attention. PFC hypoperfusion and altered connectivity are consistently reported in ME/CFS neuroimaging.

anterior cingulate cortex.label: anterior cingulate cortex
anterior cingulate cortex.definition: The ACC is a cortical region involved in error detection, conflict monitoring, pain processing, and autonomic regulation. ACC dysfunction in ME/CFS links to cognitive effort intolerance and altered pain perception.

insula.label: insula
insula.definition: A cortical region folded deep within the lateral sulcus, integrating interoceptive signals (body state awareness) with emotional and cognitive processing. Insular dysfunction in ME/CFS may explain altered interoception — the 'body feeling wrong' — and autonomic dysregulation.

amygdala.label: amygdala
amygdala.definition: An almond-shaped structure in the temporal lobe mediating fear, anxiety, and emotional salience. Amygdala hyperactivity in ME/CFS may drive hypervigilance, anxiety, and exaggerated stress responses.

hippocampus.label: hippocampus
hippocampus.definition: A seahorse-shaped structure in the medial temporal lobe critical for memory formation, spatial navigation, and HPA axis negative feedback. Reduced hippocampal volume has been reported in ME/CFS, possibly due to chronic cortisol/neuroinflammatory effects.

brainstem.label: brainstem
brainstem.definition: The stalk connecting the brain to the spinal cord, containing vital autonomic, cardiovascular, and respiratory control centers. Brainstem hypoperfusion and neuroinflammation are consistently reported in ME/CFS and may underpin orthostatic intolerance and autonomic failure.

vagus nerve.label: vagus nerve
vagus nerve.definition: Cranial nerve X: the primary parasympathetic nerve, innervating the heart, lungs, and GI tract. Vagal tone is reduced in ME/CFS, contributing to tachycardia, GI dysmotility, and impaired anti-inflammatory cholinergic reflex.

circumventricular organs.label: circumventricular organs
circumventricular organs.definition: Brain regions lacking a blood-brain barrier, allowing direct sensing of circulating signals (cytokines, hormones, osmolality). CVOs are key entry points for peripheral inflammatory signals to reach the brain in ME/CFS sickness behavior.
circumventricular organs.also: organum vasculosum of the lamina terminalis (OVLT), area postrema, subfornical organ (SFO)

hypothalamus.label: hypothalamus
hypothalamus.definition: A small but critical brain region below the thalamus controlling homeostasis: HPA axis, autonomic balance, sleep-wake cycles, temperature, hunger, and thirst. Hypothalamic dysfunction is a unifying node in ME/CFS pathology — linking sleep, endocrine, autonomic, and metabolic dysregulation.

meta-analysis.label: meta-analysis
meta-analysis.definition: A statistical synthesis of results from multiple independent studies addressing the same question, providing a pooled effect estimate with greater precision than individual studies. Meta-analyses in ME/CFS are limited by heterogeneous diagnostic criteria and small sample sizes.

systematic review.label: systematic review
systematic review.definition: A comprehensive review using explicit, reproducible methods to identify, appraise, and synthesize all relevant studies on a specific question. The gold standard for evidence synthesis in ME/CFS; NICE 2021 guidelines were informed by systematic reviews.

Mendelian randomization.label: Mendelian randomization
Mendelian randomization.definition: A method using genetic variants as instrumental variables to assess causal relationships between modifiable exposures and outcomes. MR studies in ME/CFS are emerging, using GWAS data to probe causal directions (e.g., does cytokine elevation cause fatigue, or vice versa?).

case-control.label: case-control
case-control.definition: A study design comparing individuals with a disease (cases) to those without (controls), looking retrospectively at exposures or biomarkers. The most common ME/CFS study design; limited by selection bias and reverse causation.

prospective.label: prospective
prospective.definition: A study design that follows participants forward in time from exposure to outcome. Dubbo and other post-infectious fatigue cohort studies are prospective ME/CFS designs capturing disease evolution from onset — uniquely valuable.

pacing.label: pacing
pacing.definition: An energy management strategy where the patient stays within their energy envelope, balancing activity and rest to avoid triggering PEM. Pacing is the cornerstone of ME/CFS self-management, endorsed by NICE (2021), and is fundamentally different from graded exercise therapy (GET).

brain fog.label: brain fog
brain fog.definition: A colloquial term for the cognitive dysfunction of ME/CFS: impaired working memory, attention, word-finding, processing speed, and executive function. Not a formal medical term, but universally recognized by patients and now used in research literature.

allodynia.label: allodynia
allodynia.definition: Pain caused by a stimulus that does not normally provoke pain (e.g., light touch, clothing, breeze). Allodynia in ME/CFS reflects central sensitization of pain pathways and is a hallmark of comorbid fibromyalgia.

hyperalgesia.label: hyperalgesia
hyperalgesia.definition: Increased pain sensitivity — an exaggerated pain response to a normally painful stimulus. Hyperalgesia in ME/CFS is driven by central sensitization and neuroinflammation-mediated lowering of pain thresholds.

photophobia.label: photophobia
photophobia.definition: Abnormal sensitivity or intolerance to light, a common sensory hypersensitivity symptom in ME/CFS. May reflect thalamocortical sensory gating dysfunction.

phonophobia.label: phonophobia
phonophobia.definition: Abnormal sensitivity or intolerance to sound. Sound sensitivity in ME/CFS is part of the broader sensory overload pattern and may involve impaired auditory gating (reduced P50 suppression).

hyperacusis.label: hyperacusis
hyperacusis.definition: Reduced tolerance to ordinary environmental sounds, perceived as uncomfortably loud or painful. Common in ME/CFS and linked to central auditory processing dysfunction.

energy envelope.label: energy envelope
energy envelope.definition: The range of daily activity a person can sustain without triggering PEM. Staying within the energy envelope — pacing — is the primary management strategy; exceeding it triggers the boom-bust cycle.

boom-bust cycle.label: boom-bust cycle
boom-bust cycle.definition: A self-reinforcing pattern where a patient overexerts on good days ('boom'), triggering PEM, forcing prolonged rest ('bust'), then overexerts again when feeling marginally better. Pacing aims to break this cycle.

sensory overload.label: sensory overload
sensory overload.definition: A state where multiple simultaneous sensory inputs (light, sound, touch, movement) overwhelm processing capacity, causing distress, cognitive shutdown, and PEM. Sensory gating dysfunction in ME/CFS prevents normal filtering of irrelevant stimuli.

cortisol.label: cortisol
cortisol.definition: The primary glucocorticoid stress hormone produced by the adrenal cortex. Morning cortisol is typically low or low-normal in ME/CFS (blunted HPA axis), while evening cortisol may be inappropriately elevated, flattening the diurnal rhythm.

aldosterone.label: aldosterone
aldosterone.definition: A mineralocorticoid hormone regulating sodium retention, potassium excretion, and blood volume. Low aldosterone or aldosterone resistance may contribute to hypovolemia and orthostatic intolerance in ME/CFS/POTS.

renin.label: renin
renin.definition: A kidney-derived enzyme initiating the RAAS cascade, cleaving angiotensinogen to angiotensin I. Inappropriately low renin in the setting of hypovolemia (the 'renin-aldosterone paradox') is reported in a subset of POTS/ME/CFS patients.

orexin.label: orexin
orexin.definition: Orexin (hypocretin): a hypothalamic neuropeptide promoting wakefulness, appetite, and reward-seeking. Dysregulated orexin signaling (reduced overall tone but abnormal temporal patterns) produces daytime sleepiness with nighttime wakefulness — the core of ME/CFS sleep-wake pathology.

vasopressin.label: vasopressin
vasopressin.definition: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH): a hypothalamic hormone promoting water reabsorption in the kidneys and vasoconstriction. Vasopressin dysregulation may contribute to fluid balance abnormalities in ME/CFS/POTS.

oxytocin.label: oxytocin
oxytocin.definition: A hypothalamic neuropeptide mediating social bonding, trust, and parasympathetic tone. Oxytocin has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties; low oxytocin has been hypothesized in ME/CFS.

Th1.label: Th1
Th1.definition: Type 1 T helper cells: CD4+ T cells producing IFN-γ and IL-2, driving cellular immunity against intracellular pathogens. Th1/Th2 imbalance is a long-standing hypothesis in ME/CFS; some studies find Th2 skewing with reduced Th1 function.

Th2.label: Th2
Th2.definition: Type 2 T helper cells: CD4+ T cells producing IL-4, IL-5, IL-13, driving humoral immunity and allergic responses. Th2 skewing has been reported in some ME/CFS cohorts, potentially linking to MCAS and atopic comorbidity.

Th17.label: Th17
Th17.definition: Type 17 T helper cells: CD4+ T cells producing IL-17, driving neutrophil recruitment and mucosal defense. Th17 expansion is reported in ME/CFS and may drive autoimmune pathology and gut barrier dysfunction.

Treg.label: Treg
Treg.definition: Regulatory T cells: CD4+ FOXP3+ T cells that suppress excessive immune responses and maintain self-tolerance. Reduced Treg numbers or function may permit autoimmune processes to develop in ME/CFS; reduced Treg/Th17 ratio is a reported finding.

B cell.label: B cell
B cell.definition: B lymphocytes: antibody-producing immune cells. B cell dysregulation in ME/CFS includes expanded autoreactive clones, altered subset distribution, and abnormal response to EBV-infected cells. The negative RituxME trial does not preclude B cell involvement in a subset.

microglia.label: microglia
microglia.definition: The resident immune cells of the CNS, continuously surveying the brain environment. Activated microglia in ME/CFS shift from a surveillant to a pro-inflammatory state, producing cytokines, ROS, and quinolinic acid that drive neuroinflammation.

astrocyte.label: astrocyte
astrocyte.definition: Star-shaped glial cells that support neurons metabolically, regulate extracellular ion and neurotransmitter levels, form the blood-brain barrier, and mediate glymphatic clearance via AQP4 channels. Astrocyte dysfunction in ME/CFS impairs brain energy metabolism and waste clearance.

mast cell.label: mast cell
mast cell.definition: Tissue-resident immune cells containing granules of histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins, leukotrienes, and cytokines. Inappropriate mast cell degranulation (MCAS) is a major ME/CFS comorbidity, driving flushing, itching, GI symptoms, tachycardia, and neuroinflammation.

cytokine.label: cytokine
cytokine.definition: Small signaling proteins secreted by immune cells that mediate inflammation, immune cell recruitment, and systemic responses. ME/CFS is characterized by a complex cytokine signature, not simple elevation: some are elevated (TNF-α, IL-6, IFN-γ in subsets), some are reduced (IL-10, TGF-β in subsets), and patterns vary by disease duration and severity.

CPAP.label: CPAP
CPAP.definition: Continuous Positive Airway Pressure: a device delivering constant air pressure to keep the airway open during sleep. Used in ME/CFS patients with comorbid sleep apnea; treating OSA can improve sleep quality but does not resolve ME/CFS-specific unrefreshing sleep.

LLLT.label: LLLT
LLLT.definition: Low-Level Laser Therapy (photobiomodulation): non-thermal light application (red/NIR) to tissues, proposed to enhance mitochondrial function via cytochrome c oxidase photostimulation. Experimental in ME/CFS; limited evidence.

PEMF.label: PEMF
PEMF.definition: Pulsed Electromagnetic Field therapy: application of electromagnetic fields to tissues, proposed to reduce inflammation and enhance tissue repair. Anecdotal use in ME/CFS but lacks rigorous evidence.

EMDR.label: EMDR
EMDR.definition: Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing: a psychotherapy for trauma/PTSD using bilateral stimulation. May benefit ME/CFS patients with comorbid PTSD, but does not treat core ME/CFS pathophysiology.

IACFS/ME.label: IACFS/ME
IACFS/ME.definition: International Association for CFS/ME: the primary professional organization for ME/CFS clinicians and researchers. Hosts biennial conferences and publishes the ME/CFS Primer for clinicians.

Solve ME.label: Solve ME
Solve ME.definition: Solve ME/CFS Initiative: a US-based non-profit funding research, maintaining the You + ME Patient Registry and Biobank. Originally founded as the CFIDS Association of America.

ME Association.label: ME Association
ME Association.definition: A UK-based charity providing patient support, information, and funding biomedical research into ME/CFS. Publishes the ME Medical magazine and clinical guidance for GPs.

MRC.label: MRC
MRC.definition: UK Medical Research Council: a major funder of ME/CFS biomedical research. The MRC PACE trial (2011) and its aftermath fundamentally shaped the debate about GET/CBT in ME/CFS.

NIHR.label: NIHR
NIHR.definition: UK National Institute for Health and Care Research: funds and delivers health research. The NIHR has invested in ME/CFS research including the DecodeME DNA study (largest ME/CFS GWAS) and trials of medications.

DecodeME.label: DecodeME
DecodeME.definition: The largest ME/CFS GWAS study to date (UK, >25,000 participants), searching for genetic risk variants. A collaboration between the ME Association, MRC, and NIHR; results expected to identify biological pathways underlying ME/CFS susceptibility.

lactate.label: lactate
lactate.definition: The conjugate base of lactic acid, produced during anaerobic glycolysis when pyruvate cannot enter the TCA cycle. Elevated resting and post-exertional lactate in ME/CFS indicates impaired oxidative metabolism and premature glycolytic shift.

pyruvate.label: pyruvate
pyruvate.definition: The end product of glycolysis. At the mitochondrial gateway, pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA for the TCA cycle. When PDH is inhibited (by PDK or dysfunction), pyruvate is instead reduced to lactate.

acetyl-CoA.label: acetyl-CoA
acetyl-CoA.definition: Acetyl Coenzyme A: the central metabolic intermediate, feeding the TCA cycle from carbohydrate (via PDH), fat (via β-oxidation), and protein (via amino acid degradation) metabolism. Acetyl-CoA availability limits TCA cycle flux; may be reduced in ME/CFS.

ketone.label: ketone
ketone.definition: Ketone bodies (β-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate, acetone): water-soluble fuels produced by the liver from fatty acids during carbohydrate restriction or fasting. Ketones can bypass the PDH gate, directly feeding the TCA cycle. Ketogenic metabolism has been proposed as a therapeutic strategy in ME/CFS to circumvent impaired glucose oxidation.

prostaglandin.label: prostaglandin
prostaglandin.definition: Lipid signaling molecules derived from arachidonic acid via COX enzymes. PGE2 promotes inflammation, pain, fever, and vasodilation; prostaglandin dysregulation is implicated in ME/CFS pain and vascular symptoms.

leukotriene.label: leukotriene
leukotriene.definition: Inflammatory lipid mediators derived from arachidonic acid via the LOX pathway. Cysteinyl leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4) are potent bronchoconstrictors and pro-inflammatory agents released by mast cells; leukotriene overproduction contributes to MCAS symptoms in ME/CFS.

histamine.label: histamine
histamine.definition: A biogenic amine stored in mast cell and basophil granules, released upon degranulation. Histamine mediates vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, gastric acid secretion, and wakefulness. Histamine intolerance and excess (from MCAS or reduced DAO enzyme activity) drives flushing, headache, tachycardia, and GI symptoms in ME/CFS.

SCFA.label: SCFA
SCFA.definition: Short-Chain Fatty Acids: primarily acetate, propionate, and butyrate, produced by gut bacteria fermenting dietary fiber. Butyrate is the primary energy source for colonocytes and an HDAC inhibitor with anti-inflammatory properties. Reduced SCFA-producing bacteria in ME/CFS gut dysbiosis may impair gut barrier integrity and systemic immune regulation.

epigenetics.label: epigenetics
epigenetics.definition: Heritable changes in gene expression without changes to the DNA sequence, including DNA methylation, histone modification, and non-coding RNA regulation. ME/CFS epigenetic studies reveal differential DNA methylation patterns that may explain how infections trigger persistent disease without ongoing infection.

DNA methylation.label: DNA methylation
DNA methylation.definition: The addition of methyl groups to cytosine bases in DNA (typically at CpG sites), generally silencing gene expression. Altered DNA methylation patterns in ME/CFS have been identified in immune, metabolic, and neurological pathways, including genes regulating the HPA axis and cellular energy metabolism.

hypovolemia.label: hypovolemia
hypovolemia.definition: Reduced blood volume: absolute (decreased total blood volume) or relative (normal volume but increased vascular capacity). Hypovolemia is common in ME/CFS/POTS, contributing to orthostatic intolerance, tachycardia, and reduced cerebral perfusion.

cerebral hypoperfusion.label: cerebral hypoperfusion
cerebral hypoperfusion.definition: Reduced blood flow to the brain. SPECT and MRI studies consistently demonstrate cerebral hypoperfusion in ME/CFS, particularly in the brainstem, frontal lobes, and temporal lobes, correlating with cognitive symptom severity.

microvascular.label: microvascular
microvascular.definition: Pertaining to the smallest blood vessels: arterioles, capillaries, and venules. Microvascular dysfunction in ME/CFS — impaired oxygen delivery and waste removal at the capillary level — likely contributes to muscle and brain symptoms independent of macrovascular function.

blood volume.label: blood volume
blood volume.definition: The total volume of blood in the circulatory system. ME/CFS patients often have reduced blood volume (by 10–30%), driven by low renin-aldosterone, autonomic dysfunction, and possible venous pooling, contributing substantially to orthostatic intolerance.

neuropathic pain.label: neuropathic pain
neuropathic pain.definition: Pain caused by damage or dysfunction of the somatosensory nervous system, characterized by burning, shooting, tingling, or electric shock sensations. SFN and central sensitization contribute to neuropathic pain in ME/CFS.

endotype.label: endotype
endotype.definition: A disease subtype defined by a distinct biological mechanism (rather than symptoms alone). ME/CFS is increasingly conceptualized as a collection of endotypes: autoimmune, metabolic, neuroinflammatory, hyperadrenergic, hypovolemic, connective tissue — each requiring different treatment approaches.

biomarker.label: biomarker
biomarker.definition: A measurable biological indicator of a disease state, severity, or treatment response. No validated diagnostic biomarker exists for ME/CFS, though candidates include 2-day CPET, cytokine panels, metabolomic signatures, EEG alpha-delta sleep, and microRNA profiles.

heterogeneity.label: heterogeneity
heterogeneity.definition: Variability between patients — a defining feature of ME/CFS. Clinical heterogeneity (symptom presentation, severity, triggers) reflects underlying biological heterogeneity (endotypes), explaining why no single treatment works for all patients.

falsifiability.label: falsifiability
falsifiability.definition: The capacity for a hypothesis to be proven wrong — a criterion of scientific rigor. ME/CFS research has often suffered from unfalsifiable psychological models; current biomedical research emphasizes testable mechanistic hypotheses.

autoantibody.label: autoantibody
autoantibody.definition: An antibody directed against the body's own proteins or receptors. Functional autoantibodies against GPCRs (adrenergic, muscarinic, angiotensin receptors) have been identified in subsets of ME/CFS and POTS, potentially explaining autonomic and vascular symptoms.

GPCR autoantibody.label: GPCR autoantibody
GPCR autoantibody.definition: Autoantibodies targeting G protein-coupled receptors (β-adrenergic, muscarinic acetylcholine, angiotensin II AT1R, endothelin ETA receptors), found in subsets of ME/CFS and POTS. These can be agonistic (activating) or antagonistic (blocking), directly dysregulating autonomic, cardiovascular, and immune function.

Naltrexone.label: Naltrexone
Naltrexone.definition: An opioid receptor antagonist. At standard doses (50 mg), used for opioid and alcohol dependence. At low doses (LDN, 1.5–4.5 mg), produces paradoxical anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects via transient opioid receptor blockade and TLR4 antagonism on microglia.
Naltrexone.brand: Revia, Vivitrol (standard dose); compounded for LDN
Naltrexone.rx: Prescription (US, EU); must be compounded for LDN dosing

Aripiprazole.label: Aripiprazole
Aripiprazole.definition: An atypical antipsychotic with partial D2/5-HT1A agonist and 5-HT2A antagonist activity. Used off-label at very low doses (< 2 mg) in severe ME/CFS for sensory hypersensitivity and neuroinflammation; the partial dopamine agonism may avoid the fatigue of full antagonists.

Armodafinil.label: Armodafinil
Armodafinil.definition: The R-enantiomer of modafinil, a longer-acting eugeroic (wakefulness-promoting agent) acting via dopamine reuptake inhibition. Used off-label in ME/CFS for daytime sleepiness and brain fog. Same PEM-masking risk as modafinil.

Aspirin.label: Aspirin
Aspirin.definition: Acetylsalicylic acid: an irreversible COX-1/COX-2 inhibitor NSAID with antiplatelet effects at low doses. Used in ME/CFS for pain and headache; antiplatelet effects may be relevant to microvascular dysfunction but increase bleeding risk.

Azathioprine.label: Azathioprine
Azathioprine.definition: A purine antimetabolite immunosuppressant (prodrug of 6-mercaptopurine) used in autoimmune diseases. Investigated experimentally in ME/CFS for autoimmune endotypes; requires TPMT testing before use due to myelotoxicity risk.

Diazepam.label: Diazepam
Diazepam.definition: A long-acting benzodiazepine enhancing GABA-A receptor activity. Used in ME/CFS for severe muscle spasms, anxiety, and sleep; high dependence liability and exacerbation of fatigue/brain fog limit utility.

Lorazepam.label: Lorazepam
Lorazepam.definition: An intermediate-acting benzodiazepine with no active metabolites (preferred in hepatic impairment). Used in ME/CFS for acute anxiety and sleep; carries benzodiazepine dependence risk.

Clonazepam.label: Clonazepam
Clonazepam.definition: A long-acting benzodiazepine with additional serotonergic effects. Used in ME/CFS for sleep, myoclonus, sensory hypersensitivity, and anxiety. High potency increases dependence risk; withdrawal may worsen ME/CFS symptoms.

Cyclophosphamide.label: Cyclophosphamide
Cyclophosphamide.definition: A nitrogen mustard alkylating agent immunosuppressant used in severe autoimmune disease. Investigated in a small Norwegian trial for severe ME/CFS with reported benefit; significant toxicity (hemorrhagic cystitis, malignancy, infertility) limits use to the most severe cases.

Diclofenac.label: Diclofenac
Diclofenac.definition: A non-selective NSAID with higher COX-2 selectivity than ibuprofen. Used in ME/CFS for inflammatory and joint pain; carries significant cardiovascular risk with chronic use.

Domperidone.label: Domperidone
Domperidone.definition: A peripheral D2 receptor antagonist antiemetic increasing GI motility. Used in ME/CFS for gastroparesis, nausea, and bloating. Not approved in the US (available in EU/Canada); carries risk of QT prolongation.
Domperidone.rx: Prescription (EU, Canada); not FDA-approved in US

Famciclovir.label: Famciclovir
Famciclovir.definition: A prodrug of penciclovir, an antiviral active against herpesviruses (HSV, VZV). Used off-label in ME/CFS for suspected herpesvirus reactivation; similar rationale to valacyclovir but different spectrum and pharmacokinetics.
Famciclovir.class: Antiviral / nucleoside analogue DNA polymerase inhibitor

Hydroxyzine.label: Hydroxyzine
Hydroxyzine.definition: A first-generation H1 antihistamine with strong sedative, anxiolytic, and antiemetic effects. Used in ME/CFS with MCAS for nighttime histamine symptoms and sleep initiation; anticholinergic effects may worsen brain fog.

Lamotrigine.label: Lamotrigine
Lamotrigine.definition: An anticonvulsant and mood stabilizer blocking voltage-gated sodium channels, reducing glutamate release. Used off-label in ME/CFS for neuropathic pain, sensory hypersensitivity, and mood instability.
Lamotrigine.class: Anticonvulsant / voltage-gated sodium channel blocker / mood stabilizer

Levetiracetam.label: Levetiracetam
Levetiracetam.definition: An anticonvulsant binding synaptic vesicle protein SV2A, modulating neurotransmitter release. Used off-label in ME/CFS for brain fog, sensory hypersensitivity, and cognitive dysfunction; generally well-tolerated.

Metoclopramide.label: Metoclopramide
Metoclopramide.definition: A D2 antagonist and 5-HT4 agonist prokinetic and antiemetic. Used in ME/CFS for gastroparesis and severe nausea; crosses blood-brain barrier with significant risk of extrapyramidal side effects and tardive dyskinesia.

Milnacipran.label: Milnacipran
Milnacipran.definition: An SNRI with relatively balanced serotonin/norepinephrine reuptake inhibition, FDA-approved for fibromyalgia. Used off-label in ME/CFS for pain and fatigue; more noradrenergic than most SNRIs.

Mycophenolate.label: Mycophenolate
Mycophenolate.definition: An immunosuppressant inhibiting inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase (IMPDH), blocking purine synthesis in lymphocytes. Used in autoimmune diseases; experimental in ME/CFS for autoimmune endotypes.

Nortriptyline.label: Nortriptyline
Nortriptyline.definition: The secondary amine active metabolite of amitriptyline, a tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) with less sedation and fewer anticholinergic effects. Used in ME/CFS for sleep, pain, and headache at low doses (10–50 mg).

Pantoprazole.label: Pantoprazole
Pantoprazole.definition: A proton pump inhibitor (PPI) reducing gastric acid secretion. Used in ME/CFS for GERD and GI symptoms; fewer CYP450 drug interactions than omeprazole, making it preferred in patients on multiple medications.

Paracetamol.label: Paracetamol
Paracetamol.definition: Same as Acetaminophen (US). An analgesic and antipyretic with minimal anti-inflammatory activity. Used in ME/CFS for pain and headache; generally well-tolerated but hepatotoxic at supratherapeutic doses.

Rizatriptan.label: Rizatriptan
Rizatriptan.definition: A 5-HT1B/1D receptor agonist (triptan) for acute migraine with rapid onset. Used in ME/CFS patients with comorbid migraine; faster onset than sumatriptan.

Topiramate.label: Topiramate
Topiramate.definition: An anticonvulsant with multiple mechanisms (Na+ channel block, GABA-A potentiation, AMPA/kainate antagonism, carbonic anhydrase inhibition). Used in ME/CFS for migraine prevention and weight management; cognitive side effects ('Dopamax') may worsen brain fog.
Topiramate.class: Anticonvulsant / carbonic anhydrase inhibitor / migraine prophylactic

Valproate.label: Valproate
Valproate.definition: A broad-spectrum anticonvulsant and mood stabilizer enhancing GABA and inhibiting HDAC. Used in ME/CFS for mood instability and migraine; significant metabolic side effects (weight gain, insulin resistance, PCOS) and teratogenicity limit use.

Zolpidem.label: Zolpidem
Zolpidem.definition: A non-benzodiazepine hypnotic (Z-drug) selective for α1-containing GABA-A receptors. Used in ME/CFS for sleep initiation; short half-life (~2.5 h) reduces next-day sedation but may not maintain sleep.
Zolpidem.class: Z-drug / GABA-A α1-selective positive allosteric modulator

Zopiclone.label: Zopiclone
Zopiclone.definition: A non-benzodiazepine hypnotic (Z-drug) used for sleep initiation. Longer half-life (~5 h) than zolpidem but metallic taste is a common complaint. Used in ME/CFS when sleep onset is the primary problem.
Zopiclone.rx: Prescription (EU, Canada); not FDA-approved in US; controlled substance

Eszopiclone.label: Eszopiclone
Eszopiclone.definition: The S-enantiomer of zopiclone (Z-drug), FDA-approved for sleep maintenance as well as initiation. Longer duration than zolpidem; used in ME/CFS for combined sleep-onset and sleep-maintenance insomnia.

Magnesium.label: Magnesium
Magnesium.definition: An essential mineral cofactor for >300 enzymes including ATP-dependent reactions. Magnesium deficiency is common and causes fatigue, muscle cramps, and sleep disturbance overlapping with ME/CFS. Supplementation (glycinate, citrate, threonate) is widely used.
Magnesium.brand: Various: Mg glycinate, Mg citrate, Mg threonate, Mg malate

Zinc.label: Zinc
Zinc.definition: An essential trace element cofactor for immune function, antioxidant defense (SOD, Cu/Zn-SOD), and neurotransmitter synthesis. Zinc deficiency impairs T cell function and may exacerbate ME/CFS immune dysfunction.
Zinc.brand: Zinc gluconate, Zinc picolinate, Zinc citrate

Selenium.label: Selenium
Selenium.definition: An essential trace element incorporated as selenocysteine in selenoproteins: GPX (antioxidant), TXNRD (thioredoxin reduction), iodothyronine deiodinases (thyroid hormone activation). Selenium depletion may impair antioxidant defense and thyroid function in ME/CFS.

Iron.label: Iron
Iron.definition: An essential mineral for hemoglobin (oxygen transport), mitochondrial electron transport chain complexes, and neurotransmitter synthesis. Iron deficiency anemia is one of the most common conditions mistaken for ME/CFS and must be excluded. Supplementation is indicated only with documented deficiency.
Iron.generic: Iron (ferrous sulfate, ferrous gluconate, ferrous fumarate)

Vitamin D.label: Vitamin D
Vitamin D.definition: A fat-soluble secosteroid hormone regulating calcium homeostasis, immune function, and gene expression via VDR. Vitamin D deficiency is common in housebound ME/CFS patients and may worsen immune dysfunction and fatigue.

Vitamin C.label: Vitamin C
Vitamin C.definition: Ascorbic acid: a water-soluble antioxidant regenerating vitamin E and glutathione, and a cofactor for collagen synthesis, carnitine synthesis, and norepinephrine synthesis. High-dose IV vitamin C has been proposed experimentally in ME/CFS for oxidative stress and immune support.

Vitamin B.label: Vitamin B
Vitamin B.definition: The B-complex vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B5, B6, B7, B9, B12): water-soluble cofactors essential for energy metabolism, methylation, neurotransmitter synthesis, and red blood cell production. B vitamin deficiencies mimic ME/CFS and must be excluded.
Vitamin B.also: B1 (thiamine), B2 (riboflavin), B3 (niacin), B5 (pantothenic acid), B6 (pyridoxine), B7 (biotin), B9 (folate), B12 (cobalamin)
Vitamin B.generic: B-complex vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B5, B6, B7, B9, B12)

Thiamine.label: Thiamine
Thiamine.definition: Vitamin B1: cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH), α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and transketolase — critical enzymes in glucose metabolism. Thiamine deficiency produces fatigue and lactic acidosis remarkably similar to ME/CFS; high-dose thiamine has been proposed to bypass impaired PDH function.

Riboflavin.label: Riboflavin
Riboflavin.definition: Vitamin B2: precursor to FAD and FMN, the critical redox cofactors in mitochondrial Complex I and II. Riboflavin is used in migraine prophylaxis and may support mitochondrial function in ME/CFS.

Niacin.label: Niacin
Niacin.definition: Vitamin B3: precursor to NAD+ and NADP+, the central redox and signaling coenzymes. Niacin (nicotinic acid) and nicotinamide riboside (NR) are NAD+ precursors being investigated for ME/CFS metabolic dysfunction.

Pyridoxine.label: Pyridoxine
Pyridoxine.definition: Vitamin B6: cofactor for neurotransmitter synthesis (serotonin, dopamine, GABA, norepinephrine), homocysteine metabolism, and hemoglobin synthesis. B6 is required by AADC and GAD enzymes; deficiency may impair neurotransmitter balance in ME/CFS.

Cobalamin.label: Cobalamin
Cobalamin.definition: Vitamin B12: essential for methylation, DNA synthesis, and myelin maintenance. See B12 entry for full details.

Omega-3.label: Omega-3
Omega-3.definition: Polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA) with anti-inflammatory effects: EPA competes with arachidonic acid for COX/LOX, reducing pro-inflammatory eicosanoids; DHA is a structural component of neuronal membranes and a precursor to specialized pro-resolving mediators (SPMs). Omega-3 supplementation is used in ME/CFS for neuroinflammation.
Omega-3.brand: Fish oil, Krill oil, Algal oil (vegan)

EPA.label: EPA
EPA.definition: Eicosapentaenoic Acid: an omega-3 fatty acid with anti-inflammatory effects via competition with arachidonic acid in the COX/LOX pathways, reducing pro-inflammatory prostaglandin and leukotriene production. EPA-rich fish oil is used in ME/CFS for neuroinflammation.

DHA.label: DHA
DHA.definition: Docosahexaenoic Acid: an omega-3 fatty acid and major structural component of neuronal and retinal membranes. DHA is critical for synaptic function and is a precursor to neuroprotectin D1, a specialized pro-resolving mediator. DHA is used in ME/CFS for neuroprotection.

Curcumin.label: Curcumin
Curcumin.definition: The active polyphenol in turmeric, with pleiotropic anti-inflammatory effects: NF-κB inhibition, COX/LOX inhibition, NRF2 activation, and epigenetic modulation. Poor bioavailability requires formulations with piperine or liposomal delivery. Used in ME/CFS for oxidative stress and neuroinflammation.

Resveratrol.label: Resveratrol
Resveratrol.definition: A stilbenoid polyphenol found in grapes and red wine, activating SIRT1 (NAD+-dependent deacetylase) and AMPK. Resveratrol promotes mitochondrial biogenesis via PGC-1α and may improve mitochondrial function in ME/CFS.

Quercetin.label: Quercetin
Quercetin.definition: A flavonoid polyphenol with mast cell-stabilizing, antihistamine, antioxidant (NRF2 activation), and senolytic properties. Quercetin stabilizes mast cell membranes and inhibits histamine release; used in ME/CFS with MCAS/histamine intolerance.
Quercetin.generic: Quercetin (often combined with bromelain or vitamin C for absorption)

Luteolin.label: Luteolin
Luteolin.definition: A flavonoid found in celery, parsley, and chamomile, with mast cell-stabilizing, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects. Luteolin inhibits mast cell degranulation and microglial activation; used in ME/CFS/MCAS for neuroinflammation and brain fog.
Luteolin.generic: Luteolin (often as luteolin-7-O-glucoside or liposomal luteolin)

EGCG.label: EGCG
EGCG.definition: Epigallocatechin Gallate: the major catechin in green tea, with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects. EGCG inhibits NF-κB, activates NRF2, and may reduce microglial activation; used in ME/CFS for neuroinflammation.

PQQ.label: PQQ
PQQ.definition: Pyrroloquinoline Quinone: a redox cofactor and potent antioxidant that stimulates mitochondrial biogenesis via PGC-1α and NRF1/2 pathways. PQQ acts as a growth factor for mitochondria; used in ME/CFS for mitochondrial support.

Creatine.label: Creatine
Creatine.definition: A nitrogenous compound stored as phosphocreatine in muscle and brain, serving as a rapid ATP buffer via the creatine kinase reaction. Creatine supplementation may improve muscle and cognitive energy in ME/CFS by enhancing the phosphocreatine energy shuttle.

Carnitine.label: Carnitine
Carnitine.definition: A quaternary ammonium compound essential for mitochondrial fatty acid transport via CPT1/CPT2. Acetyl-L-carnitine (ALCAR) also donates acetyl groups for acetylcholine synthesis and crosses the blood-brain barrier. Carnitine deficiency has been reported in ME/CFS.
Carnitine.class: Carnitine / mitochondrial fatty acid transport cofactor

D-Ribose.label: D-Ribose
D-Ribose.definition: A pentose sugar that is the structural backbone of ATP. D-ribose supplementation bypasses the rate-limiting pentose phosphate pathway, potentially accelerating ATP regeneration. Used in ME/CFS for energy support; mixed evidence from clinical trials.

Glutathione.label: Glutathione
Glutathione.definition: The body's master intracellular antioxidant: a tripeptide (glutamate-cysteine-glycine) that directly neutralizes ROS, recycles other antioxidants (vitamins C & E), and detoxifies xenobiotics via GST enzymes. Glutathione depletion is a core finding in ME/CFS. Oral bioavailability is poor; liposomal and IV forms are used clinically.

ALA.label: ALA
ALA.definition: Alpha-Lipoic Acid (thioctic acid): a mitochondrial cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and a potent amphipathic antioxidant (active in both aqueous and lipid compartments). ALA regenerates glutathione, vitamin C, and vitamin E; used in ME/CFS for oxidative stress and mitochondrial support. Not to be confused with alpha-linolenic acid (also ALA).

SAMe.label: SAMe
SAMe.definition: S-Adenosyl Methionine: the universal methyl donor for DNA methylation, neurotransmitter synthesis, and creatine synthesis. SAMe is used in ME/CFS for methylation support and mood; elevated homocysteine + low SAMe suggest impaired methylation cycle.

Taurine.label: Taurine
Taurine.definition: A sulfur-containing amino acid that is not incorporated into proteins. Taurine stabilizes cell membranes, regulates calcium handling, acts as an osmolyte, and enhances GABAergic tone. Used in ME/CFS for sleep, muscle cramps, and cardiac function.

Lysine.label: Lysine
Lysine.definition: An essential amino acid that competes with arginine for cellular uptake, inhibiting herpesvirus replication (which requires arginine-rich proteins). Used in ME/CFS with suspected HSV/EBV reactivation for viral suppression.
Lysine.class: Essential amino acid / antiviral (competitive arginine antagonism)

Glycine.label: Glycine
Glycine.definition: The simplest amino acid, acting as an inhibitory neurotransmitter (glycine receptor) and NMDA receptor co-agonist. Glycine is a precursor to glutathione (with glutamate + cysteine) and collagen. Used in ME/CFS for sleep quality and connective tissue support.

Arginine.label: Arginine
Arginine.definition: A conditionally essential amino acid and the substrate for nitric oxide synthase (NOS). Arginine supplementation may enhance NO-mediated vasodilation, potentially improving microvascular perfusion in ME/CFS. Note: arginine can feed herpesvirus replication.

Citrulline.label: Citrulline
Citrulline.definition: A non-proteinogenic amino acid that is converted to arginine in the kidneys, providing a more sustained and better-tolerated arginine source than arginine itself. Used in ME/CFS for NO-mediated vasodilation without the GI side effects of arginine.
Citrulline.class: Amino acid / NO precursor (indirect, via arginine)

Tryptophan.label: Tryptophan
Tryptophan.definition: An essential amino acid and precursor to serotonin (via TPH) and melatonin (via AANAT). In ME/CFS, tryptophan is diverted away from serotonin and toward the kynurenine pathway by IDO/TDO activation, depleting serotonin. Tryptophan supplementation may be counterproductive when IDO is activated.
Tryptophan.class: Essential amino acid / serotonin and melatonin precursor

5-HTP.label: 5-HTP
5-HTP.definition: 5-Hydroxytryptophan: the direct precursor to serotonin (5-HT), bypassing the rate-limiting TPH step. Used in ME/CFS for mood, sleep, and pain; converted peripherally to serotonin in the gut, with only a fraction reaching the CNS. Caution with SSRIs/SNRIs due to serotonin syndrome risk.

Tyrosine.label: Tyrosine
Tyrosine.definition: A conditionally essential amino acid and precursor to catecholamines (dopamine → norepinephrine → epinephrine) via TH and AADC. Used in ME/CFS for cognitive function and fatigue when catecholamine depletion is suspected; caution in hyperadrenergic states.

Theanine.label: Theanine
Theanine.definition: L-Theanine: a non-proteinogenic amino acid from green tea that crosses the BBB and increases GABA, serotonin, and dopamine levels while promoting alpha brainwave activity (relaxed alertness). Used in ME/CFS for anxiety reduction without sedation and for improving sleep quality.

Ashwagandha.label: Ashwagandha
Ashwagandha.definition: Withania somnifera: an adaptogenic herb used in Ayurveda with cortisol-modulating, GABAergic, and antioxidant effects. Reduces stress-induced cortisol elevation but may lower already-low cortisol in ME/CFS; used for sleep and anxiety.
Ashwagandha.generic: Ashwagandha root extract (Withania somnifera, standardized to withanolides)

Rhodiola.label: Rhodiola
Rhodiola.definition: Rhodiola rosea: an adaptogenic herb used for fatigue, cognitive performance, and stress resilience. Rhodiola may enhance mitochondrial function and modulate serotonin/dopamine. Used in ME/CFS for fatigue and brain fog; activating effect may be poorly tolerated in severe ME/CFS.
Rhodiola.generic: Rhodiola rosea root extract (standardized to rosavins and salidroside)

Cordyceps.label: Cordyceps
Cordyceps.definition: A medicinal mushroom (Cordyceps militaris or C. sinensis) used in traditional Chinese medicine for fatigue and respiratory conditions. Cordyceps contains cordycepin and polysaccharides with immunomodulatory, antioxidant, and mitochondrial-supportive effects. Used in ME/CFS for energy and immune modulation.
Cordyceps.generic: Cordyceps militaris or C. sinensis extract (standardized to cordycepin/polysaccharides)

Lion's Mane.label: Lion's Mane
Lion's Mane.definition: Hericium erinaceus: a medicinal mushroom that stimulates nerve growth factor (NGF) synthesis and promotes neurogenesis. Used in ME/CFS for cognitive dysfunction and peripheral neuropathy; proposed to improve brain fog via NGF-mediated neuronal repair.

Reishi.label: Reishi
Reishi.definition: Ganoderma lucidum: a medicinal mushroom with immunomodulatory polysaccharides (β-glucans) and triterpenes. Reishi has anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and mast cell-stabilizing effects. Used in ME/CFS for immune balance and MCAS symptoms.
Reishi.class: Medicinal mushroom / immunomodulator / mast cell stabilizer

CBD.label: CBD
CBD.definition: Cannabidiol: a non-psychoactive cannabinoid from cannabis/hemp with anti-inflammatory, analgesic, anxiolytic, and anticonvulsant effects. CBD acts on serotonin (5-HT1A agonist), TRPV1, PPARγ, and adenosine receptors. Used in ME/CFS for pain, sleep, anxiety, and neuroinflammation.
CBD.brand: Epidiolex (FDA-approved for epilepsy); various supplement brands

THC.label: THC
THC.definition: Delta-9-Tetrahydrocannabinol: the primary psychoactive cannabinoid in cannabis, acting on CB1 and CB2 receptors. Used medically for pain, nausea, appetite stimulation, and sleep. Caution in ME/CFS: may worsen orthostatic intolerance (vasodilation), brain fog, and tachyphylaxis.
THC.brand: Marinol, Syndros (synthetic THC); various medical cannabis products

Ketamine.label: Ketamine
Ketamine.definition: An NMDA receptor antagonist dissociative anesthetic with rapid-acting antidepressant effects. Low-dose ketamine infusions are used experimentally in ME/CFS and fibromyalgia for central sensitization, neuropathic pain, and treatment-resistant depression. The mechanism involves mTOR-mediated synaptogenesis via AMPA activation.
Ketamine.brand: Ketalar, Spravato (esketamine, nasal spray, FDA-approved for depression)
Ketamine.class: NMDA receptor antagonist / dissociative anesthetic / rapid-acting antidepressant
Ketamine.rx: Prescription only; administered in clinic (IV infusion) or supervised (nasal Spravato)

Psilocybin.label: Psilocybin
Psilocybin.definition: A serotonergic psychedelic (prodrug of psilocin, a 5-HT2A agonist) under investigation for depression, anxiety, and cluster headaches. Hypothesized to promote neuroplasticity and disrupt maladaptive neural networks. Not yet studied in ME/CFS; potential relevance to central sensitization and treatment-resistant depression.
Psilocybin.rx: Investigational; Schedule I in US; clinical trial use only. Decriminalized/medical use in limited jurisdictions.
Psilocybin.brand: Investigational — no commercial brand for depression
